differential_diagnoses: Dabrafenib and trametinib-induced systemic adverse reaction (pyrexia syndrome and drug-induced liver injury)
Biliary obstruction (e.g., sphincter of Oddi dysfunction or occult choledocholithiasis)
next_steps: Discontinue all non-essential medications and evaluate for drug-induced liver injury
Perform peripheral blood smear for detection of Anaplasma morulae in neutrophils
Order infectious workup including serum ferritin, soluble IL-2 receptor, and triglycerides to evaluate for HLH
Obtain MRCP (Magnetic Resonance Cholangiopancreatography) to rule out occult biliary disease
Perform liver biopsy for histopathological evaluation
Consult Hepatology and Infectious Disease specialists
Consider systemic corticosteroid therapy if drug-induced systemic inflammatory response is confirmed

differential_diagnoses: Dabrafenib and trametinib-induced systemic pyrexia syndrome and associated drug-induced liver injury (DILI)
Hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH) secondary to drug-induced inflammatory response
Biliary pathology including occult choledocholithiasis or sphincter of Oddi dysfunction
next_steps: Perform diagnostic laboratory panel: serum ferritin, triglycerides, fibrinogen, LDH, and soluble IL-2 receptor levels
Perform peripheral blood smear for evaluation of cytopenias and search for infectious inclusions or schistocytes
Obtain MRCP (Magnetic Resonance Cholangiopancreatography) to evaluate the biliary tree for choledocholithiasis
Obtain autoimmune serology (ANA, ASMA, IgG) to rule out autoimmune hepatitis
Obtain cardiac evaluation (ECG and high-sensitivity troponin) to monitor for cardiotoxicity associated with MAPK inhibitors
Defer liver biopsy until coagulation profile is optimized and less invasive diagnostic options are exhausted
Maintain strict cessation of dabrafenib and trametinib
Consult specialized hepatology and infectious disease teams for management of potential drug-induced immune-related adverse events
